payload.aggregated_output:
# Validation Problem #val-2k
# Validation case for deuterium release from self-irradiated tungsten with
# natural and artificial oxide layers based on:
# Kremer, K., Brucker, M., Jacob, W., Schwarz-Selinger, T. (2022)
# "Influence of thin surface oxide films on hydrogen isotope release from ion-irradiated tungsten"
#
# This companion stage adds the explicit 5 nm oxide-layer comparison while
# leaving the tungsten-only natural-oxide baseline untouched.
# Included physics:
# - dimensionless deuterium diffusion in a front 5 nm oxide layer and tungsten substrate
# - six scaled val-2f trap families introduced through SpeciesTrapping physics blocks in the tungsten
# - phenomenological D2 and D2O surface release on the front oxide surface and back tungsten surface
# Deferred to later stages:
# - explicit hydrogen-containing species
# - water formation
# - oxide reduction

!include parameters_val-2k_5nm_oxide.params
!include val-2k_5nm_oxide_layer.i
!include val-2k_traps_5nm_oxide.i
!include val-2k_surface_5nm_oxide.i

[Mesh]
  active = mesh_fine
  [mesh_fine]
    type = CartesianMeshGenerator
    dim = 1
    dx = '${oxide_thickness_hat} ${left_refined_tungsten_depth_hat} ${damage_remainder_depth_hat} ${oxide_buffer_fine_depth_hat} ${oxide_buffer_coarse_depth_hat} ${oxide_bulk_depth_hat}'
    ix = '${ix_oxide_fine} ${ix_left_refined_tungsten_fine} ${ix_damage_remainder_fine} ${ix_buffer_fine} ${ix_buffer_coarse_fine} ${ix_bulk_fine}'
    subdomain_id = '0 1 1 1 1 1'
  []
  [mesh_coarse]
    type = CartesianMeshGenerator
    dim = 1
    dx = '${oxide_thickness_hat} ${left_refined_tungsten_depth_hat} ${damage_remainder_depth_hat} ${oxide_buffer_fine_depth_hat} ${oxide_buffer_coarse_depth_hat} ${oxide_bulk_depth_hat}'
    ix = '${ix_oxide_coarse} ${ix_left_refined_tungsten_coarse} ${ix_damage_remainder_coarse} ${ix_buffer_coarse} ${ix_buffer_coarse_coarse} ${ix_bulk_coarse}'
    subdomain_id = '0 1 1 1 1 1'
  []
[]

[Variables]
  [deuterium_mobile]
    order = FIRST
    family = LAGRANGE
    initial_condition = ${initial_mobile_concentration_hat}
  []
[]

[AuxVariables]
  active = 'bounds_dummy temperature deuterium_mobile_physical deuterium_trapped_intrinsic_physical
  deuterium_trapped_1_physical deuterium_trapped_2_physical deuterium_trapped_3_physical
  deuterium_trapped_4_physical deuterium_trapped_5_physical deuterium_total_physical'
  [bounds_dummy]
    order = FIRST
    family = LAGRANGE
  []
  [temperature]
    initial_condition = ${temperature_initial}
  []
  [deuterium_mobile_physical]
  []
  [deuterium_trapped_intrinsic_physical]
    initial_condition = 0
  []
  [deuterium_trapped_1_physical]
    initial_condition = 0
  []
  [deuterium_trapped_2_physical]
    initial_condition = 0
  []
  [deuterium_trapped_3_physical]
    initial_condition = 0
  []
  [deuterium_trapped_4_physical]
    initial_condition = 0
  []
  [deuterium_trapped_5_physical]
    initial_condition = 0
  []
  [deuterium_total_physical]
  []
[]

[Bounds]
  [deuterium_mobile_lower_bound]
    type = ConstantBounds
    variable = bounds_dummy
    bounded_variable = deuterium_mobile
    bound_type = lower
    bound_value = 0
  []
[]

[Kernels]
  [mobile_time]
    type = TimeDerivative
    variable = deuterium_mobile
  []
  [mobile_diffusion]
    type = ADMatDiffusion
    variable = deuterium_mobile
    diffusivity = diffusivity_mobile
  []
[]

[AuxKernels]
  [temperature_aux]
    type = FunctionAux
    variable = temperature
    function = temperature_history
    execute_on = 'INITIAL LINEAR'
  []
  [deuterium_mobile_physical_aux]
    type = NormalizationAux
    variable = deuterium_mobile_physical
    normal_factor = ${mobile_concentration_reference_m3}
    source_variable = deuterium_mobile
    execute_on = 'INITIAL TIMESTEP_END'
  []
  [deuterium_trapped_intrinsic_physical_aux]
    type = NormalizationAux
    block = 1
    variable = deuterium_trapped_intrinsic_physical
    normal_factor = ${trap_concentration_reference_intrinsic_m3}
    source_variable = deuterium_trapped_intrinsic
    execute_on = 'INITIAL TIMESTEP_END'
  []
  [deuterium_trapped_1_physical_aux]
    type = NormalizationAux
    block = 1
    variable = deuterium_trapped_1_physical
    normal_factor = ${trap_concentration_reference_1_m3}
    source_variable = deuterium_trapped_1
    execute_on = 'INITIAL TIMESTEP_END'
  []
  [deuterium_trapped_2_physical_aux]
    type = NormalizationAux
    block = 1
    variable = deuterium_trapped_2_physical
    normal_factor = ${trap_concentration_reference_2_m3}
    source_variable = deuterium_trapped_2
    execute_on = 'INITIAL TIMESTEP_END'
  []
  [deuterium_trapped_3_physical_aux]
    type = NormalizationAux
    block = 1
    variable = deuterium_trapped_3_physical
    normal_factor = ${trap_concentration_reference_3_m3}
    source_variable = deuterium_trapped_3
    execute_on = 'INITIAL TIMESTEP_END'
  []
  [deuterium_trapped_4_physical_aux]
    type = NormalizationAux
    block = 1
    variable = deuterium_trapped_4_physical
    normal_factor = ${trap_concentration_reference_4_m3}
    source_variable = deuterium_trapped_4
    execute_on = 'INITIAL TIMESTEP_END'
  []
  [deuterium_trapped_5_physical_aux]
    type = NormalizationAux
    block = 1
    variable = deuterium_trapped_5_physical
    normal_factor = ${trap_concentration_reference_5_m3}
    source_variable = deuterium_trapped_5
    execute_on = 'INITIAL TIMESTEP_END'
  []
  [deuterium_total_physical_aux]
    type = ParsedAux
    variable = deuterium_total_physical
    coupled_variables = 'deuterium_mobile_physical deuterium_trapped_intrinsic_physical
    deuterium_trapped_1_physical deuterium_trapped_2_physical deuterium_trapped_3_physical
    deuterium_trapped_4_physical deuterium_trapped_5_physical'
    expression = 'deuterium_mobile_physical + deuterium_trapped_intrinsic_physical +
    deuterium_trapped_1_physical + deuterium_trapped_2_physical + deuterium_trapped_3_physical +
    deuterium_trapped_4_physical + deuterium_trapped_5_physical'
    execute_on = 'INITIAL TIMESTEP_END'
  []
[]

[Functions]
  [temperature_history]
    type = PiecewiseLinear
    data_file = gold/Experimental_desorption_temperature.csv
    format = columns
    x_title = 'time (s)'
    y_title = 'Temperature (K)'
  []
[]

[Postprocessors]
  [temperature_pps]
    type = ElementAverageValue
    variable = temperature
    execute_on = 'INITIAL TIMESTEP_END'
  []
  [integral_mobile]
    type = ElementIntegralVariablePostprocessor
    variable = deuterium_mobile
    outputs = none
  []
  [scaled_mobile]
    type = ScalePostprocessor
    scaling_factor = '${fparse mobile_concentration_reference * length_reference * ${units 1 m^2 -> mum^2}}'
    value = integral_mobile
  []
  [mobile_inventory_physical]
    type = ScalePostprocessor
    scaling_factor = 1
    value = scaled_mobile
  []
  [flux_surface_left_d2]
    type = ADSideAverageMaterialProperty
    boundary = left
    property = flux_recombination_surface
    outputs = none
  []
  [scaled_flux_surface_left_d2]
    type = ScalePostprocessor
    scaling_factor = '${fparse -1 * mobile_concentration_reference * length_reference * ${units 1 m^2 -> mum^2} / time_reference}'
    value = flux_surface_left_d2
    execute_on = 'INITIAL LINEAR NONLINEAR TIMESTEP_END'
  []
  [flux_surface_left_d2_physical]
    type = ScalePostprocessor
    scaling_factor = 1
    value = scaled_flux_surface_left_d2
  []
  [flux_surface_left_d2o]
    type = ADSideAverageMaterialProperty
    boundary = left
    property = flux_recombination_surface_d2o
    outputs = none
  []
  [scaled_flux_surface_left_d2o]
    type = ScalePostprocessor
    scaling_factor = '${fparse -1 * mobile_concentration_reference * length_reference * ${units 1 m^2 -> mum^2} / time_reference}'
    value = flux_surface_left_d2o
    execute_on = 'INITIAL LINEAR NONLINEAR TIMESTEP_END'
  []

# This file extends the shared val-2k parameters for the explicit 5 nm oxide case.
# Unit system:
# - length: micrometers in the input file
# - time: seconds
# - concentration: atoms / micrometer^3
# - flux: atoms / micrometer^2 / second

!include parameters_val-2k_shared.params

# Oxide geometry and transport
oxide_thickness = '${units 5 nm -> mum}'
left_refined_total_depth = '${fparse 2 * oxide_thickness}'
left_refined_tungsten_depth = '${fparse left_refined_total_depth - oxide_thickness}'
oxide_buffer_fine_depth = '${fparse left_refined_depth - oxide_thickness - damage_depth}'
oxide_buffer_coarse_depth = '${fparse buffer_depth - oxide_buffer_fine_depth}'
oxide_bulk_depth = '${fparse sample_thickness - oxide_thickness - damage_depth - oxide_buffer_fine_depth - oxide_buffer_coarse_depth}'

ix_oxide_fine = 15
ix_left_refined_tungsten_fine = 15
ix_damage_remainder_fine = '${fparse ix_damage_fine - ix_left_refined_tungsten_fine}'
ix_oxide_coarse = 3
ix_left_refined_tungsten_coarse = 3
ix_damage_remainder_coarse = '${fparse ix_damage_coarse - ix_left_refined_tungsten_coarse}'

diffusion_oxide_preexponential = ${diffusion_W_preexponential} ####'${units 1e-10 m^2/s -> mum^2/s}'
diffusion_oxide_energy = '${diffusion_W_energy}'

oxide_thickness_hat = '${fparse oxide_thickness / length_reference}'
left_refined_tungsten_depth_hat = '${fparse left_refined_tungsten_depth / length_reference}'
damage_remainder_depth_hat = '${fparse damage_depth_hat - left_refined_tungsten_depth_hat}'
oxide_buffer_fine_depth_hat = '${fparse oxide_buffer_fine_depth / length_reference}'
oxide_buffer_coarse_depth_hat = '${fparse oxide_buffer_coarse_depth / length_reference}'
oxide_bulk_depth_hat = '${fparse oxide_bulk_depth / length_reference}'
diffusion_oxide_preexponential_hat = '${fparse diffusion_oxide_preexponential * time_reference / (length_reference ^ 2)}'

    source_variable = deuterium_trapped_intrinsic
    execute_on = 'INITIAL TIMESTEP_END'
  []
  [deuterium_trapped_1_physical_aux]
    type = NormalizationAux
    block = 1
    variable = deuterium_trapped_1_physical
    normal_factor = ${trap_concentration_reference_1_m3}
    source_variable = deuterium_trapped_1
    execute_on = 'INITIAL TIMESTEP_END'
  []
  [deuterium_trapped_2_physical_aux]

  []
  [deuterium_trapped_3_physical_aux]

    execute_on = 'INITIAL TIMESTEP_END'
  []
  [deuterium_total_physical_aux]
    type = ParsedAux
    variable = deuterium_total_physical
    coupled_variables = 'deuterium_mobile_physical deuterium_trapped_intrinsic_physical
    deuterium_trapped_1_physical deuterium_trapped_2_physical deuterium_trapped_3_physical
    deuterium_trapped_4_physical deuterium_trapped_5_physical'
    expression = 'deuterium_mobile_physical + deuterium_trapped_intrinsic_physical +
    deuterium_trapped_1_physical + deuterium_trapped_2_physical + deuterium_trapped_3_physical +

    type = ElementAverageValue

    execute_on = 'INITIAL TIMESTEP_END'
  []

    type = ElementIntegralVariablePostprocessor
    variable = deuterium_mobile
    outputs = none
  []
  [scaled_mobile]
    type = ScalePostprocessor
    scaling_factor = '${fparse mobile_concentration_reference * length_reference * ${units 1 m^2 -> mum^2}}'
    value = integral_mobile
  []
  [mobile_inventory_physical]
    type = ScalePostprocessor
    scaling_factor = 1
    value = scaled_mobile
  []
  [flux_surface_left_d2]
    type = ADSideAverageMaterialProperty
    boundary = left
    property = flux_recombination_surface
    outputs = none
  []
  [scaled_flux_surface_left_d2]
    type = ScalePostprocessor
    scaling_factor = '${fparse -1 * mobile_concentration_reference * length_reference * ${units 1 m^2 -> mum^2} / time_reference}'
    value = flux_surface_left_d2

payload.message: I’ve confirmed the current 5 nm case is still a two-block surrogate with an uncoupled D2O surface-loss channel. # introducing the trapped species through SpeciesTrapping physics blocks with
# dimensionless concentrations, restricted to the tungsten block.

[Bounds]
  [deuterium_trapped_intrinsic_lower_bound]
    type = ConstantBounds
    variable = bounds_dummy
    bounded_variable = deuterium_trapped_intrinsic
    bound_type = lower
    bound_value = 0
  []
  [deuterium_trapped_1_lower_bound]
    type = ConstantBounds
    variable = bounds_dummy
    bounded_variable = deuterium_trapped_1
    bound_type = lower

  []
  [deuterium_trapped_4_lower_bound]
    type = ConstantBounds
    variable = bounds_dummy
    bounded_variable = deuterium_trapped_4
    bound_type = lower
    bound_value = 0
  []
  [deuterium_trapped_5_lower_bound]
    type = ConstantBounds
    variable = bounds_dummy
    bounded_variable = deuterium_trapped_5
    bound_type = lower
    bound_value = 0
  []
[]

[Functions]
  [trap_intrinsic_sites_function]
    type = ParsedFunction
    expression = '${trapping_site_fraction_intrinsic}'
  []
  [trap_1_sites_function]
    type = ParsedFunction
    expression = '${trapping_site_fraction_1} / (1 + exp((x - ${depth_center_hat}) / ${depth_width_hat}))'
  []
  [trap_2_sites_function]
    type = ParsedFunction
    expression = '${trapping_site_fraction_2} / (1 + exp((x - ${depth_center_hat}) / ${depth_width_hat}))'

  [trap_3_sites_function]
    type = ParsedFunction
    expression = '${trapping_site_fraction_3} / (1 + exp((x - ${depth_center_hat}) / ${depth_width_hat}))'

  [trap_4_sites_function]

  []

  [initial_deuterium_trapped_intrinsic]
    type = ParsedFunction
    symbol_names = 'trap_sites_function'
    symbol_values = 'trap_intrinsic_sites_function'
    expression = 'trap_sites_function * ${tungsten_density} / ${trap_concentration_reference_intrinsic}'
  []
  [initial_deuterium_trapped_1]
    type = ParsedFunction
    symbol_names = 'trap_sites_function'

  []

doc/content/verification_and_validation/val-2g.md:10:In proton-conducting ceramics, hydrogen isotopes are transported as protonic defects (OD$^{ ullet}$) through a hopping mechanism between oxygen sites in the crystal lattice. Unlike metals where hydrogen diffuses as interstitial atoms, hydrogen in PCC is incorporated into the oxygen sublattice. This case models the TDS experiment where a BZY10 sample (0.5 mm thick, 7.7 mm $	imes$ 2.2 mm surface area) is exposed to either D$_2$ gas (dry condition) or D$_2$O vapor (wet condition), followed by thermal desorption analysis.
doc/content/verification_and_validation/val-2g.md:30:In BZY10, three mobile species are considered: protonic defects (OD$^{ ullet}$), oxygen vacancies (V$_{	ext{O}}^{ ullet ullet}$), and electrons (e$'$). The hydrogen isotope transport model for PCCs considers diffusion, trapping within the material, and kinetic chemical reactions on the surface. These transport mechanisms are illustrated in [val-2g_transport_schematic].
doc/content/verification_and_validation/val-2k.md:33:Both desorption calculations start from the same preloaded tungsten state and follow the digitized temperature history from `Experimental_desorption_temperature.csv`, which heats the sample from about 296 K to about 1001 K over roughly 4.17 h. This stepwise setup isolates the contribution of the explicit oxide layer from the already-established tungsten diffusion, trapping, and surface release model. A phenomenological D$_2$O surface-loss channel is now included in parallel with the existing D$_2$ release channel, but it does not yet consume or evolve an explicit oxygen inventory.
doc/content/verification_and_validation/val-2k.md:92:The same phenomenological form is also used for a D$_2$O surface-loss channel with its own Arrhenius parameters. In the current stage those D$_2$O parameters are set equal to the D$_2$ values, which means the water-release mechanism is present structurally but still assumes an effectively constant oxygen supply at the surface. The companion 5 nm oxide layer is modeled here as a constant barrier with effective thickness and Arrhenius diffusivity parameters. No oxide-specific traps, no partition coefficient, and no oxide reduction are included yet. Both input files write physical-unit auxiliary variables and postprocessors for the mobile and trapped deuterium populations, and the comparison script reads those physical outputs directly when generating the TDS, inventory, and initial-profile figures. This remains a deliberate simplification for the current stage: the tungsten-only surrogate is preserved as the original baseline, the explicit 5 nm oxide geometry is added only as a controlled next comparison, and the new D$_2$O channel is a phenomenological release mechanism rather than a mechanistic oxide-reduction model.
doc/content/verification_and_validation/val-2j.md:51:Only O$^{-}$-center (hydroxyl group) trapping is included in this model. As noted by [!cite](kobayashi2015developing) (p. 26), tritium release controlled by detrapping from F$^+$-centers (oxygen vacancies) occurs near 580 K, which corresponds to the release temperature controlled by the diffusion process itself. Because F$^+$-center detrapping is not rate-limiting relative to diffusion, it does not produce a distinct feature (i.e., peak) in the TDS spectrum, and is therefore excluded from the model.

2	
3	# Tritium Thermal Desorption Spectroscopy from Li$_2$TiO$_3$ Solid Breeder
4	
5	## Case Description
6	
7	This validation case models tritium thermal desorption spectroscopy (TDS) from neutron-irradiated Li$_2$TiO$_3$ (lithium titanate) crystalline grains, a candidate solid tritium breeding material for Deuterium-Tritium (D-T) fusion reactors. The experimental data and model are from [!cite](kobayashi2015developing).
8	
9	Li$_2$TiO$_3$ samples were irradiated at the Kyoto University Reactor (KUR) at various neutron fluences. After irradiation, the tritium release behavior was measured by TDS with a heating rate of 5 K/min starting from 300 K using pure helium as the purge gas. The average grain radius was 1.5 $\mu$m.
10	
11	Sample E (high defect density, $D_{id}$ = $3.384 \times 10^{26}$ m$^{-3}$, corresponding to 0.018 defect/Li$_2$TiO$_3$) is modeled, where tritium release is significantly influenced by trapping at O$^{-}$-centers and defect annihilation.
12	
13	## Model Description
14	
15	### Diffusion of Mobile Species
16	
17	TMAP8 simulates tritium diffusion and trapping in a single spherical grain of Li$_2$TiO$_3$ using 1D spherical coordinates (`coord_type = RSPHERICAL`). The governing equation for mobile tritium concentration $C$ is [!citep](kobayashi2015developing):
18	
19	\begin{equation} \label{eq:diffusion_trapping}
20	\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \left( \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial r^2} + \frac{2}{r} \frac{\partial C}{\partial r} \right) - \frac{\partial C_T}{\partial t},
21	\end{equation}
22	
23	where $C_T$ is the concentration of trapped tritium in O$^{-}$-centers, $D$ is the temperature-dependent diffusivity following the Arrhenius law:
24	
25	\begin{equation} \label{eq:diffusivity}
26	D = D_0 \exp \left( -\frac{E_d}{k_B T} \right),
27	\end{equation}
28	
29	where $D_0$ is the pre-exponential factor, $E_d$ is the activation energy, $k_B$ is the Boltzmann constant, and $T$ is the temperature.
30	
31	### Trapping and Detrapping
32	
33	Only O$^{-}$-center (hydroxyl group) trapping is included in this model. As noted by [!cite](kobayashi2015developing) (p. 26), tritium release controlled by detrapping from F$^+$-centers (oxygen vacancies) occurs near 580 K, which corresponds to the release temperature controlled by the diffusion process itself. Because F$^+$-center detrapping is not rate-limiting relative to diffusion, it does not produce a distinct feature (i.e., peak) in the TDS spectrum, and is therefore excluded from the model.
34	
35	The trapped concentration $C_T$ evolves according to:
36	
37	\begin{equation}  \label{eq:trapping}
38	\frac{\partial C_T}{\partial t} = \alpha_t \frac{C_T^{\text{empty}} C_M}{N} - \alpha_r C_T,
39	\end{equation}
40	
41	where $N$ is the lattice site density, and $C_T^{\text{empty}} = \chi N - C_T$ is the empty trap concentration with $\chi$ being the trap site fraction.
42	
43	The trapping and detrapping rate coefficients follow Arrhenius relationships:
44	
45	\begin{equation} \label{eq:trapping_rate}
46	\alpha_t = \alpha_{t0} \exp\left(-\frac{\epsilon_t}{k_B T}\right),
47	\end{equation}
48	
49	\begin{equation} \label{eq:detrapping_rate}
50	\alpha_r = \alpha_{r0} \exp\left(-\frac{\epsilon_r}{k_B T}\right),
51	\end{equation}
52	
53	where $\alpha_{t0}$ and $\alpha_{r0}$ are pre-factors of trapping and release rate coefficients, $\epsilon_t$ and $\epsilon_r$ are the trapping and release energies, and $k_B$ is the Boltzmann constant.
54	
55	### Defect annihilation
56	
57	During TDS heating, radiation-induced defect sites undergo first-order annihilation [!citep](kobayashi2015developing):
58	
59	\begin{equation} \label{eq:annihilation}
60	\frac{d D_{id}}{dt} = -k_{dp-da} \, D_{id},
61	\end{equation}
62	
63	where $D_{id}$ is the defect density. The trap site fraction $\chi$ is related to radiation defect density $D_{id}$. However, the exact relationship is not clearly indicated in [!citep](kobayashi2015developing). Therefore, the initial trap site density is assumed to equal the defect density with $\chi(0)N = D_{id}$.
64	
65	The annihilation rate coefficient, $k_{dp-da}$, is described as:
66	
67	\begin{equation} \label{eq:annihilation_rate}
68	k_{dp-da} = k_{dp-da,0} \exp \left( -\frac{E_{dp-da}}{k_B T} \right).
69	\end{equation}
70	
71	The raising temperature reduces the available trap sites: the trap site fraction $\chi$ decays over time following [eq:annihilation], preventing re-trapping into annihilated sites. This is implemented by solving the annihilation equation self-consistently as an additional variable within the simulation, using a `ReleasingNodalKernel`.
72	
73	### Boundary and initial conditions
74	
75	- $\partial C / \partial r = 0$ at $r = 0$ (symmetry at grain center)
76	- $C = 0$ at $r = r_g$ (fast surface release, [!cite](kobayashi2015developing))
77	
78	The mobile and trapped tritium concentrations are initialized at their local trapping/detrapping equilibrium values at the starting temperature $T_{\text{start}}$ = 300 K. The equilibrium mobile concentration is computed from the balance of trapping and detrapping rates. This avoids an initial transient from any imbalance between trapping and detrapping. 
79	Since the TDS output is normalized to arbitrary units, only the shape of the release curve matters, not the absolute concentrations.
80	
81	## Case and Model Parameters
82	
83	The model parameters are summarized in [val-2j_parameters].
84	
85	!table id=val-2j_parameters caption=Values of model parameters.
86	| Parameter | Description | Value | Units | Reference |
87	| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
88	| $r_g$ | Grain radius | 1.5 | $\mu$m | [!cite](kobayashi2015developing) |
89	| $D_0$ | Diffusivity pre-exponential | 6.9 $\times 10^{-7}$ | m$^2$/s | [!cite](kobayashi2015developing), Eq. 11 |
90	| $E_d$ | Diffusion activation energy | 1.07 | eV | [!cite](kobayashi2015developing), Eq. 11 |
91	| $\alpha_{r0}$ | detrapping prefactor | 4.1 $\times 10^{6}$ | s$^{-1}$ | [!cite](kobayashi2015developing), Eq. 13 |

payload.message: One modeling choice needs to be explicit before I patch the inputs: I’m going to initialize the oxide oxygen field from the experimental oxide inventory rather than from a generic `WO3` density. The cleanest paper-based value I found is the same authors’ measured oxygen areal density of `1.0e21 O/m^2` over `13.5 nm`, which implies a uniform oxide oxygen concentration of about `7.4e28 O/m^3`; I’ll use that as the default unless the release paper itself exposes a tighter value while I’m wiring the transport.

content.text: One modeling choice needs to be explicit before I patch the inputs: I’m going to initialize the oxide oxygen field from the experimental oxide inventory rather than from a generic `WO3` density. The cleanest paper-based value I found is the same authors’ measured oxygen areal density of `1.0e21 O/m^2` over `13.5 nm`, which implies a uniform oxide oxygen concentration of about `7.4e28 O/m^3`; I’ll use that as the default unless the release paper itself exposes a tighter value while I’m wiring the transport.

# This file provides the quantities shared by the val-2k tungsten-only and
# explicit-oxide inputs.
# Unit system:
# - length: micrometers in the input file
# - time: seconds
# - concentration: atoms / micrometer^3
# - flux: atoms / micrometer^2 / second

# Physical constants
kb = '${units 1.380649e-23 J/K}' # Boltzmann constant from PhysicalConstants.h
eV_to_J = '${units 1.602176634e-19 J/eV}' # Electron-volt to Joule conversion from PhysicalConstants.h
kb_eV = '${units ${fparse kb / eV_to_J} eV/K}'

# Shared geometry
sample_thickness = '${units 0.8e-3 m -> mum}'
damage_depth = '${units 2.3e-6 m -> mum}'
left_refined_depth = '${units 8 mum}'
buffer_depth = '${units 20e-6 m -> mum}'
profile_depth = '${units 3.1 mum}'
profile_num_points = 163

# Shared tungsten mesh settings
ix_damage_fine = 120
ix_buffer_fine = 120
ix_buffer_coarse_fine = 34
ix_bulk_fine = 180
ix_damage_coarse = 40
ix_buffer_coarse = 40
ix_buffer_coarse_coarse = 12
ix_bulk_coarse = 60

# Temperature history
temperature_initial = '${units 295.7746478873239 K}'
end_time = '${units 4.166189111747851 h -> s}'

# Material properties
tungsten_density = '${units 6.3222e28 at/m^3 -> at/mum^3}'

# Tritium diffusion and trapping
diffusion_W_preexponential = '${units 1.6e-7 m^2/s -> mum^2/s}'
diffusion_W_energy = '${units 0.28 eV}'
trapping_energy = '${fparse ${units ${diffusion_W_energy} eV -> J} / kb}'
trapping_energy_intrinsic = '${fparse trapping_energy}'
trapping_energy_1 = '${fparse trapping_energy}'
trapping_energy_2 = '${fparse trapping_energy}'
trapping_energy_3 = '${fparse trapping_energy}'
trapping_energy_4 = '${fparse trapping_energy}'
trapping_energy_5 = '${fparse trapping_energy}'
trapping_prefactor = '${units ${fparse ${units ${diffusion_W_preexponential} mum^2/s -> m^2/s} / (1.1e-10^2 * 6)} 1/s}'
detrapping_prefactor = '${units 1e13 1/s}'
detrapping_prefactor_intrinsic = '${fparse detrapping_prefactor}'
detrapping_prefactor_1 = '${fparse detrapping_prefactor}'
detrapping_prefactor_2 = '${fparse detrapping_prefactor}'
detrapping_prefactor_3 = '${fparse detrapping_prefactor}'
detrapping_prefactor_4 = '${fparse detrapping_prefactor}'
detrapping_prefactor_5 = '${fparse detrapping_prefactor}'
detrapping_energy_1 = '${fparse ${units 1.15 eV -> J} / kb}'
detrapping_energy_2 = '${fparse ${units 1.35 eV -> J} / kb}'
detrapping_energy_3 = '${fparse ${units 1.65 eV -> J} / kb}'
detrapping_energy_4 = '${fparse ${units 1.85 eV -> J} / kb}'
detrapping_energy_5 = '${fparse ${units 2.05 eV -> J} / kb}'
detrapping_energy_intrinsic = '${fparse ${units 1.04 eV -> J} / kb}'

# Surface recombination
recombination_coefficient = '${units 3.8e-16 m^4/at/s -> mum^4/at/s}'
recombination_energy = '${units 0.34 eV}'
d2o_recombination_coefficient = '${fparse recombination_coefficient}'
d2o_recombination_energy = '${fparse recombination_energy}'

# val-2f six-trap reference scaled to preserve the current val-2k initial areal inventory
depth_center = '${units 2.3e-6 m -> mum}'
depth_width = '${units 5e-8 m -> mum}'
trap_density_scale = 6.6448483834798155 # (/)
trapping_site_fraction_intrinsic = '${fparse trap_density_scale * 3.796146910885451e-07}' # (/)
trapping_site_fraction_1 = '${fparse trap_density_scale * 7.322741092695964e-04}' # (/)
trapping_site_fraction_2 = '${fparse trap_density_scale * 4.5458706307839917e-04}' # (/)
trapping_site_fraction_3 = '${fparse trap_density_scale * 3.103067322529255e-04}' # (/)
trapping_site_fraction_4 = '${fparse trap_density_scale * 4.6932472533804296e-04}' # (/)
trapping_site_fraction_5 = '${fparse trap_density_scale * 1.2447228505500194e-04}' # (/)

# Initial mobile concentration
initial_mobile_concentration = '${units 1e-20 at/m^3 -> at/mum^3}'

# Dimensionless references and groups, following the val-2f formulation.
length_reference = '${units 1 mum}'
time_reference = '${units 1 s}'
mobile_concentration_reference = '${units 6.3222e16 at/m^3 -> at/mum^3}'
trap_concentration_reference_intrinsic = '${units 6.3222e17 at/m^3 -> at/mum^3}'
trap_concentration_reference_1 = '${units 6.3222e20 at/m^3 -> at/mum^3}'
trap_concentration_reference_2 = '${units 6.3222e20 at/m^3 -> at/mum^3}'
trap_concentration_reference_3 = '${units 6.3222e20 at/m^3 -> at/mum^3}'
trap_concentration_reference_4 = '${units 6.3222e20 at/m^3 -> at/mum^3}'
trap_concentration_reference_5 = '${units 6.3222e20 at/m^3 -> at/mum^3}'
mobile_concentration_reference_m3 = '${units 6.3222e16 at/m^3}'
trap_concentration_reference_intrinsic_m3 = '${units 6.3222e17 at/m^3}'
trap_concentration_reference_1_m3 = '${units 6.3222e20 at/m^3}'
trap_concentration_reference_2_m3 = '${units 6.3222e20 at/m^3}'
trap_concentration_reference_3_m3 = '${units 6.3222e20 at/m^3}'
trap_concentration_reference_4_m3 = '${units 6.3222e20 at/m^3}'
trap_concentration_reference_5_m3 = '${units 6.3222e20 at/m^3}'

initial_mobile_concentration_hat = '${fparse initial_mobile_concentration / mobile_concentration_reference}'
damage_depth_hat = '${fparse damage_depth / length_reference}'
profile_depth_hat = '${fparse profile_depth / length_reference}'
end_time_hat = '${fparse end_time / time_reference}'
depth_center_hat = '${fparse depth_center / length_reference}'
depth_width_hat = '${fparse depth_width / length_reference}'

dimensionless_trapping_rate_coefficient_intrinsic = '${fparse trapping_prefactor * time_reference * mobile_concentration_reference / tungsten_density}'
dimensionless_trapping_rate_coefficient_1 = '${fparse trapping_prefactor * time_reference * mobile_concentration_reference / tungsten_density}'
dimensionless_trapping_rate_coefficient_2 = '${fparse trapping_prefactor * time_reference * mobile_concentration_reference / tungsten_density}'
dimensionless_trapping_rate_coefficient_3 = '${fparse trapping_prefactor * time_reference * mobile_concentration_reference / tungsten_density}'
dimensionless_trapping_rate_coefficient_4 = '${fparse trapping_prefactor * time_reference * mobile_concentration_reference / tungsten_density}'
dimensionless_trapping_rate_coefficient_5 = '${fparse trapping_prefactor * time_reference * mobile_concentration_reference / tungsten_density}'

dimensionless_release_rate_coefficient_intrinsic = '${fparse detrapping_prefactor_intrinsic * time_reference}'
dimensionless_release_rate_coefficient_1 = '${fparse detrapping_prefactor_1 * time_reference}'
dimensionless_release_rate_coefficient_2 = '${fparse detrapping_prefactor_2 * time_reference}'
dimensionless_release_rate_coefficient_3 = '${fparse detrapping_prefactor_3 * time_reference}'
dimensionless_release_rate_coefficient_4 = '${fparse detrapping_prefactor_4 * time_reference}'
dimensionless_release_rate_coefficient_5 = '${fparse detrapping_prefactor_5 * time_reference}'

diffusion_W_preexponential_hat = '${fparse diffusion_W_preexponential * time_reference / (length_reference ^ 2)}'
recombination_coefficient_hat = '${fparse recombination_coefficient * mobile_concentration_reference * time_reference / length_reference}'
d2o_recombination_coefficient_hat = '${fparse d2o_recombination_coefficient * mobile_concentration_reference * time_reference / length_reference}'

[Tests]
  design = 'SpeciesTrappingPhysics.md MatNeumannBC.md'
  issues = '#399'
  validation = 'val-2k.md'
  [val-2k_implicit_euler_csvdiff]
    type = CSVDiff
    input = val-2k.i
    cli_args = 'Executioner/scheme=implicit-euler
                Outputs/file_base=val-2k_implicit_euler_out'
    csvdiff = val-2k_implicit_euler_out.csv
    heavy = true
    requirement = 'The system shall be able to model deuterium transport in self-irradiated tungsten with a natural oxide layer during a TDS experiment using deuterium diffusion, trapping, and phenomenological D2 and D2O surface release, using the implicit Euler time integration scheme.'
  []
  [val-2k_implicit_euler_exodiff]
    type = Exodiff
    input = val-2k.i
    exodiff = val-2k_implicit_euler_out.e
    prereq = val-2k_implicit_euler_csvdiff
    should_execute = false
    heavy = true
    requirement = 'The system shall be able to produce the full field solution of a simulation of deuterium transport in self-irradiated tungsten with a natural oxide layer during a TDS experiment using deuterium diffusion, trapping, and phenomenological D2 and D2O surface release, using the implicit Euler time integration scheme.'
  []
  [val-2k_bdf2_csvdiff]
    type = CSVDiff
    input = val-2k.i
    cli_args = 'Outputs/file_base=val-2k_out'
    csvdiff = val-2k_out.csv
    heavy = true
    requirement = 'The system shall be able to model deuterium transport in self-irradiated tungsten with a natural oxide layer during a TDS experiment using deuterium diffusion, trapping, and phenomenological D2 and D2O surface release, using the backwards differencing formula of second order (BDF-2) time integration scheme.'
  []
  [val-2k_bdf2_initial_profile_csvdiff]
    type = CSVDiff
    input = val-2k.i
    csvdiff = val-2k_profile_initial_out_line_profile_0000.csv
    prereq = val-2k_bdf2_csvdiff
    should_execute = false # this test relies on the output files from val-2k_bdf2_csvdiff, so it shouldn't be run twice
    heavy = true
    requirement = 'The system shall be able to output the initial deuterium concentration profile for the val-2k natural-oxide baseline, including the mobile and trapped populations used to initialize the desorption calculation.'
  []
  [val-2k_comparison]
    type = RunCommand
    command = 'python3 comparison_val-2k.py'
    requirement = 'The system shall be able to generate comparison plots for the val-2k validation, comparing the tungsten-only and 5 nm oxide-layer D2 and D2O release cases against the experimental data.'
    required_python_packages = 'matplotlib numpy pandas os'
  []
  [val-2k_5nm_oxide_bdf2_csvdiff]
    type = CSVDiff
    input = val-2k_5nm_oxide.i
    csvdiff = val-2k_5nm_oxide_out.csv
    heavy = true
    requirement = 'The system shall be able to model deuterium transport in self-irradiated tungsten with an explicit 5 nm oxide layer during a TDS experiment using deuterium diffusion, trapping, and phenomenological D2 and D2O surface release, using the backwards differencing formula of second order (BDF-2) time integration scheme.'
  []
  [val-2k_5nm_oxide_exodiff]
    type = Exodiff
    input = val-2k_5nm_oxide.i
    exodiff = val-2k_5nm_oxide_out.e
    prereq = val-2k_5nm_oxide_bdf2_csvdiff
    should_execute = false
    heavy = true
    requirement = 'The system shall be able to produce the full field solution of a simulation of deuterium transport in self-irradiated tungsten with an explicit 5 nm oxide layer during a TDS experiment using deuterium diffusion, trapping, and phenomenological D2 and D2O surface release.'
  []
  [val-2k_5nm_oxide_initial_profile_csvdiff]
    type = CSVDiff
    input = val-2k_5nm_oxide.i
    csvdiff = val-2k_5nm_oxide_profile_initial_out_line_profile_0000.csv
    prereq = val-2k_5nm_oxide_bdf2_csvdiff
    should_execute = false
    heavy = true
    requirement = 'The system shall be able to output the initial deuterium concentration profile for the explicit 5 nm oxide companion case in val-2k, including the mobile and trapped populations used to initialize the desorption calculation.'
  []
[]

doc/content/verification_and_validation/val-2g.md:10:In proton-conducting ceramics, hydrogen isotopes are transported as protonic defects (OD$^{ ullet}$) through a hopping mechanism between oxygen sites in the crystal lattice. Unlike metals where hydrogen diffuses as interstitial atoms, hydrogen in PCC is incorporated into the oxygen sublattice. This case models the TDS experiment where a BZY10 sample (0.5 mm thick, 7.7 mm $	imes$ 2.2 mm surface area) is exposed to either D$_2$ gas (dry condition) or D$_2$O vapor (wet condition), followed by thermal desorption analysis.
doc/content/verification_and_validation/val-2g.md:30:In BZY10, three mobile species are considered: protonic defects (OD$^{ ullet}$), oxygen vacancies (V$_{	ext{O}}^{ ullet ullet}$), and electrons (e$'$). The hydrogen isotope transport model for PCCs considers diffusion, trapping within the material, and kinetic chemical reactions on the surface. These transport mechanisms are illustrated in [val-2g_transport_schematic].
doc/content/verification_and_validation/val-2j.md:51:Only O$^{-}$-center (hydroxyl group) trapping is included in this model. As noted by [!cite](kobayashi2015developing) (p. 26), tritium release controlled by detrapping from F$^+$-centers (oxygen vacancies) occurs near 580 K, which corresponds to the release temperature controlled by the diffusion process itself. Because F$^+$-center detrapping is not rate-limiting relative to diffusion, it does not produce a distinct feature (i.e., peak) in the TDS spectrum, and is therefore excluded from the model.

 ## Case Description
 
-This validation case models tritium thermal desorption spectroscopy (TDS) from neutron-irradiated Li$_2$TiO$_3$ (lithium titanate) crystalline grains, a candidate solid tritium breeding material for D-T fusion reactors. The experimental data and model are from [!cite](kobayashi2015developing).
+This validation case models tritium thermal desorption spectroscopy (TDS) from neutron-irradiated Li$_2$TiO$_3$ (lithium titanate) crystalline grains, a candidate solid tritium breeding material for Deuterium-Tritium (D-T) fusion reactors. The experimental data and model are from [!cite](kobayashi2015developing).
 
 Li$_2$TiO$_3$ samples were irradiated at the Kyoto University Reactor (KUR) at various neutron fluences. After irradiation, the tritium release behavior was measured by TDS with a heating rate of 5 K/min using pure helium as the purge gas. The average grain radius was 1.5 $\mu$m.
 
@@ -12,40 +12,45 @@ Sample E (high defect density, $D_{id}$ = 0.018 defect/Li$_2$TiO$_3$) is modeled
 
 ## Model Description
 
-TMAP8 simulates tritium diffusion and trapping in a single spherical grain of Li$_2$TiO$_3$ using 1D spherical coordinates (RSPHERICAL). The governing equation for mobile tritium concentration $C$ is [!citep](kobayashi2015developing):
+### Diffusion of Mobile Species
+
+TMAP8 simulates tritium diffusion and trapping in a single spherical grain of Li$_2$TiO$_3$ using 1D spherical coordinates (`coord_type = RSPHERICAL`). The governing equation for mobile tritium concentration $C$ is [!citep](kobayashi2015developing):
 
 \begin{equation} \label{eq:diffusion_trapping}
-\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \left( \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial r^2} + \frac{2}{r} \frac{\partial C}{\partial r} \right) + q_{dt} - q_{t},
+\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \left( \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial r^2} + \frac{2}{r} \frac{\partial C}{\partial r} \right) - \frac{\partial C_T}{\partial t},
 \end{equation}
 
-where $D$ is the temperature-dependent diffusivity following the Arrhenius law:
+where $C_T$ is the concentration of trapped tritium in O$^{-}$-centers, $D$ is the temperature-dependent diffusivity following the Arrhenius law:
 
 \begin{equation} \label{eq:diffusivity}
-D = D_0 \exp \left( -\frac{E_d}{k_B T} \right).
+D = D_0 \exp \left( -\frac{E_d}{k_B T} \right),
 \end{equation}
 
-### O$^{-}$-center trapping
+where $D_0$ is the pre-exponential factor, $E_d$ is the activation energy, $k_B$ is the Boltzmann constant, and $T$ is temperature.
+
+### Trapping and Detrapping
 
 Only O$^{-}$-center (hydroxyl group) trapping is included in this model. As noted by [!cite](kobayashi2015developing) (p. 26), tritium release controlled by detrapping from F$^+$-centers (oxygen vacancies) occurs near 580 K, which corresponds to the release temperature controlled by the diffusion process itself. Because F$^+$-center detrapping is not rate-limiting relative to diffusion, it does not produce a distinct feature in the TDS spectrum, and is therefore excluded from the model.
 
-The trapping and detrapping rates for O$^{-}$-centers are:
+The trapped concentration $C_T$ evolves according to:
 
-\begin{equation} \label{eq:trapping_rate}
-q_{t} = k_{t} C \frac{T_{t} - C_{t}}{M},
+\begin{equation}  \label{eq:trapping}
+\frac{\partial C_T}{\partial t} = \alpha_t \frac{C_T^{\text{empty}} C_M}{N} - \alpha_r C_T
 \end{equation}
 
-\begin{equation} \label{eq:detrapping_rate}
-q_{dt} = k_{dt} C_{t},
-\end{equation}
+where $N$ is the lattice site density, and $C_T^{\text{empty}} = \chi N - C_T$ is the empty trap concentration with $\chi$ being the trap site fraction.
 
-where $C_{t}$ is the trapped tritium concentration, $T_{t}$ is the trap density, $M$ is the lattice density, and the rate coefficients follow Arrhenius temperature dependence:
+The trapping and detrapping rate coefficients follow Arrhenius relationships:
 
-\begin{equation}
-k_{t} = k_{t,0} \exp \left( -\frac{E_t}{k_B T} \right), \quad
-k_{dt} = k_{dt,0} \exp \left( -\frac{E_{dt}}{k_B T} \right).
+\begin{equation} \label{eq:trapping_rate}
+\alpha_t = \alpha_{t0} \exp\left(-\frac{\epsilon_t}{k_B T}\right)
 \end{equation}
 
-The trapping rate parameters ($k_{t,0}$ and $E_t$) follow the McNabb-Foster assumption that the trapping energy is related to the diffusion activation energy.
+\begin{equation} \label{eq:detrapping_rate}
+\alpha_r = \alpha_{r0} \exp\left(-\frac{\epsilon_r}{k_B T}\right)
+\end{equation}
+
+where $\alpha_{t0}$ and $\alpha_{r0}$ are pre-factors of trapping and release rate coefficients, $\epsilon_t$ and $\epsilon_r$ are trapping and release energies, and $k_B$ is the Boltzmann constant.
 
 ### Defect annihilation
 
@@ -55,15 +60,15 @@ During TDS heating, radiation-induced defect sites undergo first-order annihilat
 \frac{d D_{id}}{dt} = -k_{dp-da} \, D_{id},
 \end{equation}
 
-where the annihilation rate coefficient is:
+where $D_{id}$ is defect density. The trap site fraction $\chi$ is related to radiation defect density $D_{id}$. However, the exact relationship haven't been indicated in [!citep](kobayashi2015developing). Therefore, the initial trap site fraction is assumed to equal the defect density with $\chi(0) = D_{id}$.
+
+The annihilation rate coefficient, k_{dp-da}, is described as:
 
 \begin{equation} \label{eq:annihilation_rate}
 k_{dp-da} = k_{dp-da,0} \exp \left( -\frac{E_{dp-da}}{k_B T} \right).
 \end{equation}
 
-This reduces the available trap sites: the trap density $T_{t}(t)$ decays over time following [eq:annihilation], preventing re-trapping into annihilated sites. This is implemented by solving the annihilation ODE self-consistently as an additional variable within the simulation, using a `ReleasingNodalKernel`.
-
-The initial trap density equals the total defect fraction: $T_t(0) = D_{id}$.
+The raising temperature reduces the available trap sites: the trap site fraction $\chi$ decays over time following [eq:annihilation], preventing re-trapping into annihilated sites. This is implemented by solving the annihilation equation self-consistently as an additional variable within the simulation, using a `ReleasingNodalKernel`.
 
 ### Boundary and initial conditions

 ## Results
 
+### Results before optimization
+
 [val-2j_comparison_e] shows the comparison between TMAP8 and the experimental TDS spectrum for Sample E (high defect density). The O$^{-}$-center trapping model with defect annihilation captures the broad release profile. The high detrapping energy of O$^{-}$-centers (1.19 eV) produces a release peak above 650 K that is distinct from the diffusion-controlled release. The defect annihilation mechanism reduces the effective trap density at high temperatures, suppressing re-trapping and allowing tritium to escape more efficiently.

        id=val-2j_comparison_e
        caption=Comparison of TMAP8 calculation with the experimental TDS data for Sample E (high defect density).
 
+### Results after optimization
+

Define `val-2k` as an incremental validation campaign, not a single final model.\n   Each increment should produce a runnable TMAP8 case, a comparison plot against the paper’s experimental data, and a short note on what new physics was added and what mismatch remains.\n\n2. Start with the natural-oxide experiment only.\n   Ignore the thick artificial oxide cases at first so the baseline physics is easier to interpret.\n\n3. Iteration 1: natural oxide, `D` in W with trapping, release only as `D2`.\n   Physics:\n   - 1D D diffusion in tungsten\n   - multiple trapping / detrapping populations in self-damaged W\n   - vacuum-side surface recombination to `D2`\n   - prescribed thermal history matching the experiment\n   Validation target:\n   - total non-oxygen-bearing release for the natural oxide case\n   Goal:\n   - establish the tungsten diffusion/trapping baseline before adding oxide chemistry\n\n4. Iteration 2: natural oxide, add explicit surface reaction pathways involving hydrogen and water species, but still without an explicit oxide subdomain.\n   Physics:\n   - keep Iteration 1 baseline\n   - add H-containing surface chemistry needed to represent `HD`, `HDO`, and `D2O` release channels in an effective surface model\n   Validation target:\n   - natural oxide data from the species-resolved release curves\n   Goal:\n   - understand how much of the natural-oxide behavior can be captured with surface chemistry alone\n\n5. Iteration 3: introduce a constant oxide layer for the natural-oxide case.\n   Physics:\n   - add a fixed thin oxide layer as a separate transport region\n   - keep oxide properties constant in time\n   - no oxide reduction yet\n   Validation target:\n   - natural oxide case again\n   Goal:\n   - separate barrier/reservoir effects from chemistry effects\n\n6. Iteration 4: extend the constant-oxide-layer model to artificial oxide thicknesses of 5 nm, 10 nm, and 15 nm.\n   Focus on Fig. 6 comparisons only:\n   - natural oxide\n   - 5 nm\n   - 10 nm\n   - 15 nm\n   Physics:\n   - same as Iteration 3\n   - vary only oxide thickness first, then calibrate oxide transport / trapping / interfacial parameters if needed\n   Goal:\n   - check whether a fixed oxide layer can reproduce the trend in peak shifts and species partitioning\n\n7. Iteration 5: assess whether constant-oxide physics is sufficient.\n   Compare all four cases side by side and document:\n   - what is captured\n   - where the model systematically fails\n   - whether failures point to missing oxide depletion / reduction physics\n\n8. Iteration 6: add oxide reduction as a moving-evolution effect only after the constant-layer model is understood.\n   Physics to add later:\n   - oxygen inventory in the oxide\n   - D-driven oxide consumption\n   - feedback of oxide depletion on transport and release products\n   This stage is deferred until the fixed-layer model is validated as far as possible.\n\n9. detrapping_energy_5 = '${fparse ${units 2.05 eV -> J} / kb}'
detrapping_energy_intrinsic = '${fparse ${units 1.04 eV -> J} / kb}'

# Surface recombination
recombination_coefficient = '${units 3.8e-16 m^4/at/s -> mum^4/at/s}'
recombination_energy = '${units 0.34 eV}'
d2o_recombination_coefficient = '${fparse recombination_coefficient}'
d2o_recombination_energy = '${fparse recombination_energy}'

# val-2f six-trap reference scaled to preserve the current val-2k initial areal inventory
depth_center = '${units 2.3e-6 m -> mum}'
depth_width = '${units 5e-8 m -> mum}'
trap_density_scale = 6.6448483834798155 # (/)
trapping_site_fraction_intrinsic = '${fparse trap_density_scale * 3.796146910885451e-07}' # (/)
trapping_site_fraction_1 = '${fparse trap_density_scale * 7.322741092695964e-04}' # (/)
trapping_site_fraction_2 = '${fparse trap_density_scale * 4.5458706307839917e-04}' # (/)
trapping_site_fraction_3 = '${fparse trap_density_scale * 3.103067322529255e-04}' # (/)
trapping_site_fraction_4 = '${fparse trap_density_scale * 4.6932472533804296e-04}' # (/)
trapping_site_fraction_5 = '${fparse trap_density_scale * 1.2447228505500194e-04}' # (/)

# Initial mobile concentration
initial_mobile_concentration = '${units 1e-20 at/m^3 -> at/mum^3}'

# Dimensionless references and groups, following the val-2f formulation.
length_reference = '${units 1 mum}'
time_reference = '${units 1 s}'
mobile_concentration_reference = '${units 6.3222e16 at/m^3 -> at/mum^3}'
trap_concentration_reference_intrinsic = '${units 6.3222e17 at/m^3 -> at/mum^3}'
trap_concentration_reference_1 = '${units 6.3222e20 at/m^3 -> at/mum^3}'
trap_concentration_reference_2 = '${units 6.3222e20 at/m^3 -> at/mum^3}'
trap_concentration_reference_3 = '${units 6.3222e20 at/m^3 -> at/mum^3}'
trap_concentration_reference_4 = '${units 6.3222e20 at/m^3 -> at/mum^3}'
trap_concentration_reference_5 = '${units 6.3222e20 at/m^3 -> at/mum^3}'
mobile_concentration_reference_m3 = '${units 6.3222e16 at/m^3}'
trap_concentration_reference_intrinsic_m3 = '${units 6.3222e17 at/m^3}'
trap_concentration_reference_1_m3 = '${units 6.3222e20 at/m^3}'
trap_concentration_reference_2_m3 = '${units 6.3222e20 at/m^3}'
trap_concentration_reference_3_m3 = '${units 6.3222e20 at/m^3}'
trap_concentration_reference_4_m3 = '${units 6.3222e20 at/m^3}'
trap_concentration_reference_5_m3 = '${units 6.3222e20 at/m^3}'

initial_mobile_concentration_hat = '${fparse initial_mobile_concentration / mobile_concentration_reference}'
damage_depth_hat = '${fparse damage_depth / length_reference}'
profile_depth_hat = '${fparse profile_depth / length_reference}'
end_time_hat = '${fparse end_time / time_reference}'
depth_center_hat = '${fparse depth_center / length_reference}'
depth_width_hat = '${fparse depth_width / length_reference}'

dimensionless_trapping_rate_coefficient_intrinsic = '${fparse trapping_prefactor * time_reference * mobile_concentration_reference / tungsten_density}'
dimensionless_trapping_rate_coefficient_1 = '${fparse trapping_prefactor * time_reference * mobile_concentration_reference / tungsten_density}'
dimensionless_trapping_rate_coefficient_2 = '${fparse trapping_prefactor * time_reference * mobile_concentration_reference / tungsten_density}'
dimensionless_trapping_rate_coefficient_3 = '${fparse trapping_prefactor * time_reference * mobile_concentration_reference / tungsten_density}'
dimensionless_trapping_rate_coefficient_4 = '${fparse trapping_prefactor * time_reference * mobile_concentration_reference / tungsten_density}'
dimensionless_trapping_rate_coefficient_5 = '${fparse trapping_prefactor * time_reference * mobile_concentration_reference / tungsten_density}'

dimensionless_release_rate_coefficient_intrinsic = '${fparse detrapping_prefactor_intrinsic * time_reference}'
dimensionless_release_rate_coefficient_1 = '${fparse detrapping_prefactor_1 * time_reference}'
dimensionless_release_rate_coefficient_2 = '${fparse detrapping_prefactor_2 * time_reference}'
dimensionless_release_rate_coefficient_3 = '${fparse detrapping_prefactor_3 * time_reference}'
dimensionless_release_rate_coefficient_4 = '${fparse detrapping_prefactor_4 * time_reference}'
dimensionless_release_rate_coefficient_5 = '${fparse detrapping_prefactor_5 * time_reference}'

diffusion_W_preexponential_hat = '${fparse diffusion_W_preexponential * time_reference / (length_reference ^ 2)}'
recombination_coefficient_hat = '${fparse recombination_coefficient * mobile_concentration_reference * time_reference / length_reference}'
d2o_recombination_coefficient_hat = '${fparse d2o_recombination_coefficient * mobile_concentration_reference * time_reference / length_reference}'

payload.message: I have enough source material to wire the oxygen field without inventing new constants: the release paper gives the oxide thickness context, and a companion paper from the same authors gives a measured oxygen areal density that converts to about `7.4e28 O/m^3`; for oxygen transport in W, a primary-source JNM abstract gives a `0.17 eV` barrier and enough information to infer a pre-exponential on the order of `8e-8 m^2/s`. I’m patching the 5 nm input around those values now, keeping the transition and depletion logic explicit in the input file instead of adding a new C++ object.

content.text: I have enough source material to wire the oxygen field without inventing new constants: the release paper gives the oxide thickness context, and a companion paper from the same authors gives a measured oxygen areal density that converts to about `7.4e28 O/m^3`; for oxygen transport in W, a primary-source JNM abstract gives a `0.17 eV` barrier and enough information to infer a pre-exponential on the order of `8e-8 m^2/s`. I’m patching the 5 nm input around those values now, keeping the transition and depletion logic explicit in the input file instead of adding a new C++ object.

content.text: /resume
What GitHub issue number should this session be associated with?
Add a postprocessor that tracks the mass conservation of D. It should track the amount within the sample and use the flux calculations to determine if mass is conserved.

Added the D mass-balance diagnostics to [val-2k.i](/Users/simopa/projects/tmap8/test/tests/val-2k/val-2k.i:330) and [val-2k_5nm_oxide.i](/Users/simopa/projects/tmap8/test/tests/val-2k/val-2k_5nm_oxide.i:300). Each case now reports `deuterium_inventory_in_sample_physical`, `deuterium_released_physical`, and `deuterium_mass_conservation_residual`, using the total D inventory in the sample plus the existing left/right surface flux postprocessors integrated over time.

I regenerated the `val-2k` CSV and Exodus gold files and verified them with `./run_tests -j4 --re val-2k --heavy`, which passed: 7 passed, 0 failed. On the refreshed outputs, the residual stays small relative to the initial inventory: about `1.3e-5` max for the natural-oxide BDF2 case, `6.8e-5` max for the 5 nm case, and `2.1e-3` max for the implicit-Euler case. The documented `hit check` command in this workspace reported `unrecognized subcommand 'check'`, so validation used direct `tmap8-opt` runs plus the heavy harness instead.

I still need the GitHub issue number before any commit or session-log step. Also, `test/tests/val-2k/gold/val-2k_out.e` is currently untracked because I generated it while refreshing baselines.
the python script should plot mass conservation over time for both cases.
add and commit
I need the GitHub issue number first. This repo requires every commit to reference the issue and include the session `.jsonl`, so I can’t make a compliant commit without `#<issue-number>`.

<permissions instructions>
Filesystem sandboxing defines which files can be read or written. `sandbox_mode` is `workspace-write`: The sandbox permits reading files, and editing files in `cwd` and `writable_roots`. Editing files in other directories requires approval. Network access is restricted.
# Escalation Requests

Commands are run outside the sandbox if they are approved by the user, or match an existing rule that allows it to run unrestricted. The command string is split into independent command segments at shell control operators, including but not limited to:

- Pipes: |
- Logical operators: &&, ||
- Command separators: ;
- Subshell boundaries: (...), $(...)

Each resulting segment is evaluated independently for sandbox restrictions and approval requirements.

Example:

git pull | tee output.txt

This is treated as two command segments:

["git", "pull"]

["tee", "output.txt"]

Commands that use more advanced shell features like redirection (>, >>, <), substitutions ($(...), ...), environment variables (FOO=bar), or wildcard patterns (*, ?) will not be evaluated against rules, to limit the scope of what an approved rule allows.

## How to request escalation

IMPORTANT: To request approval to execute a command that will require escalated privileges:

- Provide the `sandbox_permissions` parameter with the value `"require_escalated"`
- Include a short question asking the user if they want to allow the action in `justification` parameter. e.g. "Do you want to download and install dependencies for this project?"
- Optionally suggest a `prefix_rule` - this will be shown to the user with an option to persist the rule approval for future sessions.

If you run a command that is important to solving the user's query, but it fails because of sandboxing or with a likely sandbox-related network error (for example DNS/host resolution, registry/index access, or dependency download failure), rerun the command with "require_escalated". ALWAYS proceed to use the `justification` parameter - do not message the user before requesting approval for the command.

## When to request escalation

While commands are running inside the sandbox, here are some scenarios that will require escalation outside the sandbox:

- You need to run a command that writes to a directory that requires it (e.g. running tests that write to /var)
- You need to run a GUI app (e.g., open/xdg-open/osascript) to open browsers or files.
- If you run a command that is important to solving the user's query, but it fails because of sandboxing or with a likely sandbox-related network error (for example DNS/host resolution, registry/index access, or dependency download failure), rerun the command with `require_escalated`. ALWAYS proceed to use the `sandbox_permissions` and `justification` parameters. do not message the user before requesting approval for the command.
- You are about to take a potentially destructive action such as an `rm` or `git reset` that the user did not explicitly ask for.
- Be judicious with escalating, but if completing the user's request requires it, you should do so - don't try and circumvent approvals by using other tools.

## prefix_rule guidance

When choosing a `prefix_rule`, request one that will allow you to fulfill similar requests from the user in the future without re-requesting escalation. It should be categorical and reasonably scoped to similar capabilities. You should rarely pass the entire command into `prefix_rule`.

### Banned prefix_rules 
Avoid requesting overly broad prefixes that the user would be ill-advised to approve. For example, do not request ["python3"], ["python", "-"], or other similar prefixes that would allow arbitrary scripting.
NEVER provide a prefix_rule argument for destructive commands like rm.
NEVER provide a prefix_rule if your command uses a heredoc or herestring. 

### Examples
Good examples of prefixes:
- ["npm", "run", "dev"]
- ["gh", "pr", "check"]
- ["cargo", "test"]

# AGENTS.md instructions for /Users/simopa/projects/tmap8

<INSTRUCTIONS>
# AGENTS.md

## Environment
- Ask the user if you need to load a MOOSE conda environment, and if so, what the name of the conda environment is
- If `./tmap8-opt` or `run_tests` throws an error, stop and ask the user
- Pre-commit hooks handle all formatting and input file validation on every `git commit` — do not invoke `clang-format`, `black`, or `hit format` manually
- `moose/framework/contrib/hit/hit check <file>.i` is available to validate input files during development — use it before committing

## Before writing any code or input file
- Browse `doc/content/` to understand documentation standards and existing patterns before starting work
- Before writing a new C++ class, look through existing MOOSE classes in the codebase to confirm expected syntax and patterns
- Documentation in `doc/content/` may not accurately reflect current code behavior — once you identify a class to use in an input file, find its C++ implementation and read it to confirm the class does what you expect before writing the input file

## C++ code
- Every new class requires a MOOSE TestHarness test in `test/tests/` before committing
- Every new class requires a MooseDocs page following existing TMAP8 doc structure
- Never hardcode physical parameters — first check if it is already defined in `doc/content/source/utils/TMAP8PhysicalConstants`, and if not available, expose everything through `addParam`
- Code is unit-agnostic; units live in input files only

## Input files
- Before writing an input file, look through existing MOOSE and TMAP8 classes to confirm object names and parameter syntax
- Use `moose/framework/contrib/hit/hit check <file>.i` to validate before committing
- Use `./tmap8-opt -i <file>.i` to run input files
- Write parameter values in the units the reference source used; use the MOOSE units system for conversion — the units system is self-documenting and does not require inline comments
- State the unit system at the top of every input file in a comment block

## Testing
- Run tests only when `.C`, `.h`, or `test/tests/` files change
- Run only the affected module: `./run_tests -j<N> --re <module>`

## Session log
- Every session must be associated with a GitHub issue on `idaholab/TMAP8`; ask the user for the issue number at the start of the session if one is not provided, or create one if the user instructs you to
- **SQA requirement:** When creating a GitHub issue with `gh`, you must use the appropriate template from `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/`; read the available templates first, select the one that fits (e.g. `bug-report.md` for bugs, `feature-request.md` for new features or tasks), and populate all sections in the template body — passing a bare description without following the template structure is not acceptable
- Before committing, copy the current session's `.jsonl` file into `doc/content/agent/<issue-number>/`. For Claude Code sessions the file is the most recently modified `.jsonl` under `~/.claude/projects/<repo-slug>/`; for Codex sessions check the equivalent Codex session directory. Do not remove old session files already in that directory — append the new one alongside them.

## Committing
- Commit at minimum after: every input file that successfully runs, every test that passes or fails, and every new class added
- If a test or run failed, commit the failure state first (include the full error message in the commit body), then commit the fix separately
- Every commit must reference the associated GitHub issue (e.g. `Refs #<issue-number>` or `Closes #<issue-number>` as appropriate) in the commit message
- **Every commit must include an updated copy of the session `.jsonl`** — before staging, copy the current session file from `~/.claude/projects/<repo-slug>/<session-id>.jsonl` to `doc/content/agent/<issue-number>/` and stage it; this is a critical SQA requirement. Do not remove session files already present in that directory — if multiple Claude/Codex sessions contribute to the same branch and issue, all session files must be kept.
- Every commit must include these trailers:
```
Refs #<issue-number>
AI-assisted: true
AI-model: <model name>
AI-version: <model version>
Co-Authored-By: <model name> <noreply@anthropic.com>
```

## Pull requests
- If the linked issue already contains a complete Reason/Design/Impact description, the PR body should contain only the closing reference (e.g. `Closes #<issue-number>`) — do not duplicate the issue content or leave template sections empty
- If the PR introduces physics/modeling decisions, non-obvious AI reasoning, or tests not described in the issue, add those as a short addendum below the issue reference
- Never merge autonomously

## Never do
- Modify existing passing tests without explicit user instruction
- Modify the build system without explicit user instruction
- Make physics modeling decisions without flagging them to the user
- Suppress or bypass test failures

</INSTRUCTIONS>

</environment_context>

Add the oxygen as a variable in the oxide thin film instead of a block description of the oxide. For the diffusional and trapping properties, use the tanh function with a sharp gradient to transition from the W properties to the oxide properties. use the oxygen variable in the reaction for D20 release. The oxygen variable should start with a homogeneous concentration for the oxide layer and be depleted over time as D2O is released. Oxygen transport should be governed by the oxygen diffusion in W. Use the paper to figure out what the initial oxygen concentration should be.

settings.developer_instructions:
# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.

payload.user_instructions:
# AGENTS.md

## Environment
- Ask the user if you need to load a MOOSE conda environment, and if so, what the name of the conda environment is
- If `./tmap8-opt` or `run_tests` throws an error, stop and ask the user
- Pre-commit hooks handle all formatting and input file validation on every `git commit` — do not invoke `clang-format`, `black`, or `hit format` manually
- `moose/framework/contrib/hit/hit check <file>.i` is available to validate input files during development — use it before committing

## Before writing any code or input file
- Browse `doc/content/` to understand documentation standards and existing patterns before starting work
- Before writing a new C++ class, look through existing MOOSE classes in the codebase to confirm expected syntax and patterns
- Documentation in `doc/content/` may not accurately reflect current code behavior — once you identify a class to use in an input file, find its C++ implementation and read it to confirm the class does what you expect before writing the input file

## C++ code
- Every new class requires a MOOSE TestHarness test in `test/tests/` before committing
- Every new class requires a MooseDocs page following existing TMAP8 doc structure
- Never hardcode physical parameters — first check if it is already defined in `doc/content/source/utils/TMAP8PhysicalConstants`, and if not available, expose everything through `addParam`
- Code is unit-agnostic; units live in input files only

## Input files
- Before writing an input file, look through existing MOOSE and TMAP8 classes to confirm object names and parameter syntax
- Use `moose/framework/contrib/hit/hit check <file>.i` to validate before committing
- Use `./tmap8-opt -i <file>.i` to run input files
- Write parameter values in the units the reference source used; use the MOOSE units system for conversion — the units system is self-documenting and does not require inline comments
- State the unit system at the top of every input file in a comment block

## Testing
- Run tests only when `.C`, `.h`, or `test/tests/` files change
- Run only the affected module: `./run_tests -j<N> --re <module>`

## Session log
- Every session must be associated with a GitHub issue on `idaholab/TMAP8`; ask the user for the issue number at the start of the session if one is not provided, or create one if the user instructs you to
- **SQA requirement:** When creating a GitHub issue with `gh`, you must use the appropriate template from `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/`; read the available templates first, select the one that fits (e.g. `bug-report.md` for bugs, `feature-request.md` for new features or tasks), and populate all sections in the template body — passing a bare description without following the template structure is not acceptable
- Before committing, copy the current session's `.jsonl` file into `doc/content/agent/<issue-number>/`. For Claude Code sessions the file is the most recently modified `.jsonl` under `~/.claude/projects/<repo-slug>/`; for Codex sessions check the equivalent Codex session directory. Do not remove old session files already in that directory — append the new one alongside them.

## Committing
- Commit at minimum after: every input file that successfully runs, every test that passes or fails, and every new class added
- If a test or run failed, commit the failure state first (include the full error message in the commit body), then commit the fix separately
- Every commit must reference the associated GitHub issue (e.g. `Refs #<issue-number>` or `Closes #<issue-number>` as appropriate) in the commit message
- **Every commit must include an updated copy of the session `.jsonl`** — before staging, copy the current session file from `~/.claude/projects/<repo-slug>/<session-id>.jsonl` to `doc/content/agent/<issue-number>/` and stage it; this is a critical SQA requirement. Do not remove session files already present in that directory — if multiple Claude/Codex sessions contribute to the same branch and issue, all session files must be kept.
- Every commit must include these trailers:
```
Refs #<issue-number>
AI-assisted: true
AI-model: <model name>
AI-version: <model version>
Co-Authored-By: <model name> <noreply@anthropic.com>
```

## Pull requests
- If the linked issue already contains a complete Reason/Design/Impact description, the PR body should contain only the closing reference (e.g. `Closes #<issue-number>`) — do not duplicate the issue content or leave template sections empty
- If the PR introduces physics/modeling decisions, non-obvious AI reasoning, or tests not described in the issue, add those as a short addendum below the issue reference
- Never merge autonomously

## Never do
- Modify existing passing tests without explicit user instruction
- Modify the build system without explicit user instruction
- Make physics modeling decisions without flagging them to the user
- Suppress or bypass test failures
